metadata.homeDescription: Senior software engineer in Prague building dependable web products across banking, AI tooling, frontend architecture, accessibility, and full-stack TypeScript development.

hero.headline: Senior software engineer building dependable web products
hero.subheadline: I lead frontend and full-stack work across banking, AI tooling, and product teams, turning complex requirements into accessible, maintainable systems.
hero.supporting: My background spans customer operations, fraud prevention, regulatory work, IoT incident analysis, and software engineering. That path taught me to understand systems from both sides: how they are built, and what happens when real people depend on them.
hero.availability: Based in Prague. Open to selected freelance projects and future senior engineering opportunities.

work.title: Work where responsibility mattered
work.intro: Three examples of production engineering across sensitive systems, scientific tooling, and an owner-operated product. The focus is on scope, decisions, and dependable delivery.

experience.title: Experience across products and operations
experience.intro: Recent engineering roles are shown in detail. Earlier operational work is kept because it shaped how I investigate risk, communicate under pressure, and design for real-world consequences.

approach.title: Engineering approach
approach.intro: A few principles that guide architecture, implementation, review, and release decisions.

additionalWork.title: Additional work
additionalWork.intro: Smaller products, experiments, and client sites. Useful work, presented at the scale it deserves.

capabilities.title: Capabilities
capabilities.intro: The engineering areas I can take responsibility for, from product frontend and system boundaries to testing and delivery.

education.title: Education
education.intro: Focused software-engineering training supported by continued work in production codebases.

contact.title: Let's discuss the work
contact.intro: I'm based in Prague and open to selected freelance projects and future senior engineering opportunities. The best way to reach me is by email or LinkedIn.

work.title: Online-banking modernization
work.summary: Led frontend and BFF work within the modernization of an online-banking platform, helping move legacy functionality toward a micro-frontend architecture while improving API boundaries, testing, accessibility, and delivery quality.

detail.confidentialityNote: Product screenshots and source code are not publicly available. This case study therefore focuses on my role, architectural decisions, and engineering process.
problem: Legacy functionality needed to move toward a micro-frontend architecture without weakening the boundaries between the user interface and downstream banking services.
Frontend delivery depended on clear API contracts and a BFF layer that could absorb service-level complexity instead of leaking it into product code.
constraints: The product and implementation details are confidential.
Changes had to preserve integration stability while meeting testing and accessibility requirements.
responsibilities: Led frontend and BFF work within the modernization effort.
Built BFF controllers, mappers, and service orchestration.
Designed API contracts and coordinated integrations across downstream services.
Contributed unit, integration, E2E, and accessibility testing.
Improved CI/CD flow and code-review standards.
approach: Kept service-specific response shapes behind explicit mappers and contracts.
Treated accessibility and automated testing as delivery requirements rather than final-stage cleanup.
Used code review and CI feedback to keep changes understandable across frontend and backend boundaries.

decisions.title: Keep downstream complexity out of the UI
decisions.context: Multiple services exposed different response shapes and operational concerns.
decisions.decision: Use the BFF as an explicit integration boundary with controllers, mappers, and orchestration that presented stable contracts to the frontend.
decisions.tradeoff: The BFF adds code and ownership, but keeps product UI logic focused and easier to test.

decisions.title: Build quality into the migration
decisions.context: Modernization work can reproduce legacy behavior while introducing new integration and accessibility risks.
decisions.decision: Cover the work with unit, integration, and E2E tests, and include accessibility requirements in implementation and review.
decisions.outcome: The migration had clearer release checks and stronger delivery discipline.

outcomes: Helped move legacy functionality toward a micro-frontend architecture.
Established clearer API and BFF boundaries for frontend delivery.
Strengthened testing, accessibility, CI/CD, and review practices around the work.
reflection: In sensitive systems, architecture is not only component structure. It is also error semantics, ownership, traceability, and confidence in how changes are released.

work.title: AI tooling for scientific model metadata
work.summary: Built full-stack tooling for managing machine-learning model metadata and developed an AI-assisted workflow for extracting structured information from scientific PDFs, with validation and GitHub-based automation around the wider open-science workflow.

problem: Machine-learning model metadata needed a structured application and backend rather than a collection of disconnected manual steps.
Relevant information in scientific PDFs had to be extracted into a form that could be reviewed and used by the wider open-science workflow.
constraints: AI-extracted information could not be treated as automatically correct.
The application had to fit an existing GitHub-based collaboration and repository workflow.
responsibilities: Built the full-stack Next.js and TypeScript application.
Designed the database schema and backend for structured model metadata.
Developed the scientific-PDF workflow with the Vercel AI SDK.
Automated metadata validation and repository synchronization with GitHub API and Actions.
Worked directly with researchers and engineers in the open-science team.
approach: Modeled metadata explicitly so the application, validation, and repository workflow shared the same vocabulary.
Kept AI extraction inside a reviewable workflow instead of presenting model output as a final source of truth.
Used GitHub automation to connect application work with the repositories already used by the team.

decisions.title: Treat AI output as input to validation
decisions.context: Scientific PDFs vary in structure and LLM output is probabilistic.
decisions.decision: Extract structured candidates through the AI workflow, then keep validation and human review explicit.
decisions.tradeoff: Review adds friction, but avoids making unsupported metadata authoritative.

decisions.title: Integrate with the existing repository workflow
decisions.context: The open-science team already used GitHub repositories as part of its operating model.
decisions.decision: Automate validation and synchronization through the GitHub API and GitHub Actions instead of creating a separate publishing path.

outcomes: Delivered a full-stack interface and backend for structured model metadata.
Connected AI-assisted PDF analysis to explicit validation.
Reduced disconnects between application data and GitHub-based open-science workflows through automation.
reflection: AI features become dependable when the surrounding data model, validation, and ownership are designed as carefully as the model call itself.

work.title: devShark
work.summary: A learning platform for developers built as a real product rather than a UI demo. It combines structured learning content, quiz mechanics, progress tracking, and multiplayer functionality in a maintainable React and Supabase architecture.

image.alt: devShark learning dashboard with topic progress and daily challenge

problem: Developer-learning tools often separate content, practice, progress, and competition into disconnected experiences.
The product needed an architecture that could support structured topic paths, daily challenges, progress, leaderboards, and real-time multiplayer without becoming difficult to extend.
constraints: As an owner-operated product, product decisions, implementation, deployment, and maintenance share the same limited attention.
Real-time features and user data require clear authorization and data ownership.
responsibilities: Defined the product structure and learning mechanics.
Designed the React application and Supabase data model.
Implemented authentication, authorization, progress tracking, and quiz flows.
Built real-time multiplayer and leaderboard behavior.
Own deployment, maintenance, and ongoing product decisions.
approach: Separated learning content, attempts, progress, and multiplayer state into purposeful models.
Kept feature boundaries explicit so new topic paths and challenge types can be added without rewriting the core flow.
Used Supabase for integrated data, authentication, and real-time behavior while keeping application rules visible in the codebase.

decisions.title: Model learning progress separately from content
decisions.context: Content changes over time, while a user's attempts and progress need stable meaning.
decisions.decision: Keep content structure and user progress as separate concerns connected through stable identifiers.
decisions.outcome: The learning model can evolve without treating content records and user state as the same thing.

decisions.title: Treat multiplayer as a product flow
decisions.context: Real-time transport alone does not define room state, transitions, reconnect behavior, or results.
decisions.decision: Model multiplayer around explicit room and game states, with real-time updates carrying those state transitions.

outcomes: Shipped and operate a live product at devshark.app.
Combined structured learning, quizzes, progress, leaderboards, and multiplayer in one product architecture.
Built a foundation that supports ongoing product and content development.
reflection: Owning the product changes engineering priorities. Maintainability, operational simplicity, and what not to build matter as much as feature delivery.

experience.summary: Lead frontend work and help the team adopt AI tooling with clear conventions, while keeping accessibility, web standards, and maintainable delivery visible in day-to-day decisions.

Lead frontend delivery in TypeScript, React, and Next.js, including code quality and cross-browser behavior.
Maintain standards around accessibility, web standards, technical specifications, and client implementation guidance.
Model structured content in Payload CMS and improve technical and UX details across client sites.

experience.summary: Led frontend and BFF work within the modernization of an online-banking platform, helping migrate legacy functionality toward a micro-frontend architecture.
contributions: Built BFF controllers, mappers, and service orchestration.
Designed API contracts and kept integrations stable across downstream services.
Covered the work with unit, integration, and E2E tests, including accessibility requirements.
Improved CI/CD flow and code-review standards.

contributions: Designed the database schema and backend for structured model metadata.
Built the Next.js and TypeScript application used to manage the workflow.
Developed AI-assisted PDF analysis with the Vercel AI SDK and explicit validation.

experience.summary: Built the frontend of a B2B marketplace connecting charities and corporations, and carried a company rebrand into the production product.
contributions: Structured the frontend around reusable React and Tailwind CSS components.

Worked with PostgreSQL and Prisma on the product data layer.
Integrated Stripe and third-party APIs for payments, authentication, and real-time updates.

experience.summary: Investigated critical cold-chain incidents using live IoT telemetry for pharmaceutical shipments.
contributions: Triaged temperature excursions and ran root-cause analysis across shipment and sensor data.
Turned findings into compliant client resolutions, audit reports, and quality documentation.
Automated recurring parts of data processing and reporting.

experience.summary: Investigated fraud, money-laundering patterns, and user-protection risks in a regulated environment.
contributions: Reviewed betting and transaction data with dedicated analysis tools.
Handled flagged-account investigations and sensitive data under GDPR.
Worked within EU and local gaming regulation and mentored junior analysts.

experience.summary: Resolved complex booking failures across airline and CRM systems for customers and B2B partners.
contributions: Worked across multiple systems to resolve time-sensitive travel issues.
Helped improve support workflows and trained new team members.
Supported KAYAK operations and B2B partners with evidence-based resolutions.

earlierCareerIntro: Eight years in customer operations, fraud prevention, regulatory work, and cold-chain IoT support shaped how I build software today. I learned to investigate incidents, communicate under pressure, work with sensitive data, and understand the operational cost of unclear interfaces and unreliable systems.

approach.title: Dependable boundaries
approach.body: I prefer explicit contracts between frontend, backend, and external services. Stable boundaries, clear error semantics, and purposeful data models keep complexity from leaking through the application.

approach.title: Quality is part of delivery
approach.body: Accessibility, tests, performance, and maintainability are not cleanup tasks after a feature ships. They are part of how the feature is designed, reviewed, and released.

approach.title: Product context matters
approach.body: Good implementation starts with understanding who depends on the system, what can fail, and what the operational consequences are, not with choosing a framework.

approach.title: AI with guardrails
approach.body: I use AI where it improves research, delivery, and repetitive engineering work, but keep validation, tests, security boundaries, and human review explicit.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.description: How this portfolio handles optional analytics and visitor data.

sections.title: Site operator
paragraphs: This portfolio is operated by Lukas Kouril in Prague, Czech Republic. You can contact me at kouril.lukas@gmail.com.

sections.title: Optional analytics
paragraphs: When a PostHog key is configured, this site asks for consent before loading analytics. If no key is configured, no analytics interface is shown and no analytics requests are made.

Analytics is used to understand basic page visits and deliberate navigation to case studies, the CV, GitHub, or LinkedIn. Form values, pointer movement, keystrokes, copied text, and detailed scrolling are not tracked.

sections.title: Consent and preferences
paragraphs: Your choice is stored in your browser. You can change it from the privacy control in the footer. Do Not Track is respected as an additional signal and prevents analytics from loading.

sections.title: Provider and retention
paragraphs: Optional analytics is provided by PostHog using its EU service endpoint. Session recording, form autocapture, and unnecessary person profiles are disabled. Retention is controlled in the PostHog project settings and should be kept only as long as needed for basic site analytics.

sections.title: Direct contact
paragraphs: If you contact me by email or LinkedIn, those services process the information you choose to send under their own privacy terms. This site does not include a contact form or store message content.

consent.title: Optional analytics
consent.body: May I use privacy-conscious analytics to understand page visits and deliberate link navigation? The site works the same if you decline.
consent.dnt: Analytics is disabled because your browser sends Do Not Track.
